does: SHRED introduces a retain-set-free unlearning method for large language models that selectively removes memorized content without requiring curated retain examples. The core insight is that memorized knowledge concentrates in high-information (low-probability) tokens within forget instances, while low-information tokens reflect general competence. The method operates in two stages: first, it identifies high-surprisal tokens through a forward pass and computes per-token autoregressive probabilities to locate forget positions; second, it applies modified KL divergence targets to demote logits at these high-information positions while preserving predictions at benign anchor positions. This selective token-level approach enables effective unlearning while maintaining general model utility without needing a separate retain set.
connects: This work builds on the machine unlearning literature for language models, which has grappled with the tension between removing memorized content and preserving general capabilities. Prior methods, including those using knowledge distillation and entropy-based approaches, typically required retain sets of task-relevant examples to prevent utility degradation—a costly requirement that limited practical deployment. SHRED extends self-distillation techniques by introducing the observation that unlearning granularity matters: previous approaches treated entire instances uniformly, whereas this work leverages information theory to distinguish high-surprisal tokens carrying memorization from low-surprisal tokens encoding general knowledge. The method also connects to recent work on understanding which model components store specific information and how selective modification can preserve broad capabilities.
enables: This approach enables more practical large-scale unlearning systems by eliminating the retain-set dependency, reducing the data curation burden for deploying unlearning in privacy-critical and copyright-sensitive applications. The token-level analysis framework could generalize to other selective model modification tasks, such as removing specific concepts or mitigating hallucinations without full retraining. Future work might extend this insight to explore whether similar information-theoretic principles apply to unlearning in other modalities or architectures, or whether the method can be combined with active selection strategies to further optimize the forget positions chosen. The logit demotion technique also opens directions for understanding what linguistic or semantic patterns correspond to memorization versus generalization, potentially informing interpretability research.
